w:r.w:t: Based on "The Devoted Friend" by Oscar Wilde
This unit focuses on developing reading comprehension and critical thinking skills through Oscar Wilde's satirical short story,
The story explores themes of friendship, exploitation, selfishness, and the difference between words and actions.
Little Hans, an honest gardener, lives alone in a tiny cottage with a beautiful garden full of flowers.
His "most devoted friend" is Big Hugh the Miller, a wealthy man who constantly takes flowers, herbs, and fruit from Hans's garden.
The Miller preaches that "real friends should have everything in common," but never gives anything in return.
During spring, summer, and autumn, Hans is happy working in his garden.
In winter, Hans suffers from cold and hunger, with no flowers to sell.
The Miller refuses to visit Hans in winter, claiming that "when people are in trouble they should be left alone."
The Miller's wife praises his "thoughtful" ideas about friendship, while his young son shows genuine kindness (which the Miller dismisses).
When spring arrives, the Miller visits Hans, promising to give him his old, broken wheelbarrow.
In exchange, the Miller manipulates Hans into giving him a plank of wood for his barn roof and filling a large basket with primroses.
The Miller uses the promise of the wheelbarrow to justify asking Hans for favors.
The Miller repeatedly asks Hans to do chores: carrying flour to market, mending the barn roof, driving sheep to the mountain.
Each time, Hans hesitates but agrees because he believes the Miller is his "best friend" and will give him the wheelbarrow.
Hans neglects his own garden and flowers, but consoles himself with the Miller's promises.
One stormy night, the Miller comes to Hans's door, asking him to fetch the doctor for his injured son.
Hans agrees, even though the Miller refuses to lend his lantern for the dark, dangerous journey.
Hans gets lost in the storm, falls into a pool on the moor, and drowns.

At Hans's funeral, the Miller acts as the chief mourner but complains about the wheelbarrow he never actually gave: "I will certainly take care not to give away anything again. One always suffers for being generous."
The Miller claims to be Hans's best friend but only takes, never gives.
The Miller uses manipulative language and false promises to exploit Hans's kindness.
The Miller speaks beautifully about friendship but acts selfishly.
Hans's simplicity and trust make him vulnerable to manipulation.
Wilde uses humor and irony to criticize hypocritical morality.
The victim of false friendship; represents innocence and generosity.
The antagonist; uses words to justify exploitation.
Contrasts with his father; represents true kindness.
Minor character who highlights the urgency of the final request.
Q1: How does the friendship between Hans and the Miller seem odd from the beginning?

The friendship is one-sided. The Miller constantly takes flowers, herbs, and fruit from Hans's garden but never gives anything in return. He preaches that "real friends should have everything in common," yet he keeps all his wealth (flour, cows, sheep) to himself while Hans suffers.
Q2: Why did Hans sell his wheelbarrow?

Hans sold his wheelbarrow because winter was very hard for him. He had no money to buy bread, so he first sold his silver buttons, then his silver chain, then his big pipe, and finally his wheelbarrow to survive.
Q3: In what way does the Miller exploit Hans with the promise of giving him his wheelbarrow?

The Miller uses the promise of the broken wheelbarrow to manipulate Hans into giving him a plank of wood, filling a large basket with flowers, carrying flour to market, mending the barn roof, and driving sheep to the mountain—all without ever actually giving Hans the wheelbarrow.
Q4: What final act of the Miller's selfishness leads to Hans' death?

On a stormy night, the Miller asks Hans to fetch the doctor for his injured son but refuses to lend his lantern, saying it is "new" and might get damaged. Hans goes into the dark, dangerous storm without light, gets lost on the moor, and drowns.
Q5: Hans' own simplicity and trustworthiness also contribute to his death. Do you agree? Justify.

Yes. Hans blindly trusts the Miller's words despite clear evidence of exploitation. He repeatedly neglects his own needs and safety because he believes the Miller's promises. His inability to question false friendship and set boundaries makes him vulnerable to the Miller's manipulation.
Q6: "False friends are worse than open enemies." Discuss this aphorism in the context of the story.

An open enemy would not deceive Hans with kind words. The Miller, as a "false friend," gains Hans's trust and then exploits him. Hans never defends himself because he believes the Miller cares for him. This betrayal is more dangerous than open hostility because it disarms the victim emotionally.
Q7: How would you deal if you have a friend like the Miller?

I would observe whether the friendship is reciprocal. If a friend only takes and never gives, or uses guilt and manipulation, I would set clear boundaries, communicate my feelings, and if necessary, distance myself. True friendship requires mutual respect and support.
Q8: Write any five contrasting characteristics of Hans and the Miller.
Uses "friendship" as a tool for exploitation
B) In a tiny cottage by himself
C) A variety of beautiful flowers and herbs
3. What did the Miller often say about friendship?
A) "Friends should compete with each other."
B) "Real friends should have everything in common."
C) "Friendship is a waste of time."
D) "Never trust a friend with your belongings."
4. Why didn't the Miller visit Hans in winter?
A) He was too busy with his mill
B) He believed people in trouble should be left alone
5. What did the Miller promise to give Hans?
6. What did the Miller ask for in return for the wheelbarrow?
B) A plank of wood and a basket of primroses
7. Which chore did the Miller NOT ask Hans to do?
A) Carry a sack of flour to market
C) Gather wood for the kitchen fire
D) Drive sheep to the mountain
8. Why did Hans agree to do all the Miller's requests?
A) He was afraid of the Miller
B) He believed the Miller was his best friend and would give him the wheelbarrow
C) The Miller paid him money
D) He had nothing else to do
9. What happened on the stormy night?
A) The Miller invited Hans to dinner
B) The Miller asked Hans to fetch the doctor for his injured son
C) Hans visited the Miller to complain
D) A fire broke out at the mill
A) He was attacked by robbers
B) He fell into a pool on the moor after getting lost in the storm
C) He caught a cold from the Miller
D) He was tired from working and fell asleep
11. What did the Miller say at Hans's funeral?
C) "I had as good as given him my wheelbarrow… One always suffers for being generous."
D) "I should have helped him more."
12. What is the main moral of the story?
A) Always help your friends, no matter the cost
D) Winter is the hardest season
13. What does "by-the-bye" mean in the story?
14. What does "getting on" mean in context?
15. What does "took down" mean when Hans "took down" the Miller's words?
16. Which statement BEST describes the Miller's view of friendship?
A) Friendship requires sacrifice from both sides
B) Friendship means sharing everything equally
C) Friendship is a tool to get what you want while appearing generous
D) Friendship is only for good weather
17. Why is the story considered satirical?
A) It makes fun of gardening
B) It uses humor and irony to criticize hypocritical morality and false friendship
C) It is a scary ghost story
D) It teaches how to build a wheelbarrow
18. What lesson can readers learn from Hans's character?
B) Kindness should be balanced with self-respect and critical thinking
D) Sell your belongings in winter
19. How does Oscar Wilde use dialogue to develop characters?
A) The Miller's eloquent speeches reveal his hypocrisy; Hans's simple replies show his innocence
B) All characters speak the same way
C) Dialogue is not used in the story
20. Which quote BEST shows the Miller's selfishness?
A) "Real friends should have everything in common."
B) "Flour is one thing, and friendship is another, and they should not be confused."
C) "I am his best friend, and I will always watch over him."
D) "One always suffers for being generous."
(Ironically, he says this after causing Hans's death)
"Real friends should have everything in common."
Shows hypocrisy—he takes everything, gives nothing
"When people are in trouble they should be left alone."
Excuse for abandoning Hans in winter
Final ironic statement after causing Hans's death
The Miller claims to be generous but is selfish.
Criticizes false morality and hypocritical friendship.
Always support answers with evidence from the text.
For "moral of the story" questions, focus on reciprocity in friendship.
When analyzing characters, compare words vs. actions.
Use quotes to strengthen critical thinking answers.
